text:
Thousands of spectators heading to theParis Olympicscould be caught up in severe travel disruptions following a "massive arson attack" on France's rail network.

Just hours before the much-anticipatedopening ceremonyalong the River Seine, vandals struck the TGV high-speed train network. Officials have reported that the culprits set fire to crucial installations, leading to the cancellation of numerous TGV services, including those transporting fans to Paris.

Transport Minister Patrice Vergriete has spoken out about thefires, confirming that they were the result of "coordinated malicious acts" carried out on Friday. He also highlighted the significant impact this will have on the travel plans of many French citizens during the holiday period.

The first fire was spotted early Friday morning near the railway tracks at Courtalain, located in the Eure-et-Loir department, disrupting services on the busy Atlantique high-speed line. SNCF, the national rail company of France, has also mentioned a "malicious act" affecting the high-speed route between Lille and Paris, particularly in the Arras area.

This led to TGV Inoui and Ouigo trains being rerouted onto regular lines, resulting in further cancellations and extended travel times. The chaos was evident at key Parisian stations like Montparnasse, where departures to and from towns such as Tours and Le Mans were halted, reportsthe Mirror.

The Gare du Nord Paris' central hub for the Eurostar has been struck by a series of attacks on the rail network, an SNCF spokesman confirmed. "Numerous stations are affected, including Garde du Nord, with services across France cancelled or delayed," he revealed.

With the Olympics kicking off, thousands from the UK are expected to travel to Paris by train today.

Additionally, the spokesman disclosed that the network had suffered "a massive arson attack to paralyze the TGV network". He warned that the disruption is likely to "last at least the whole weekend" and could disrupt services network-wide.

Authorities have launched criminal investigations into the sabotage, although there's no immediate lead on who might be responsible. Olympic officials have been wary of potential sabotage targeting the multi-billion-pound event, prompting a colossal £350million security operation in Paris.

France is witnessing its largest peacetime security force mobilisation, with around 75,000 military personnel, police, and private security agents deployed. General Lionel Catar, one of the military planners, assured, "We're focused and we're ready," ahead of the grand opening.

The main concern is safeguarding approximately 80,000 athletes as they parade along the River Seine on barges, observed by an estimated 350,000 onlookers and over a billion TV viewers. Gerald Darmanin, the French Interior Minister, stressed that "the terrorist threat remains high" and maintaining "a high level of vigilance" is crucial.

title: Paris Olympics chaos as 'massive attack' hits France rail network hours before opening ceremony - Daily Record
author: Peter Allen
description: The TGV high speed train network was targeted by vandals just hours before the opening ceremony of the Paris Olympics.
